verdict_reasoning: BAX is a cytoplasmic pro-apoptotic BCL-2 family member, canonically localized to the cytosol and outer mitochondrial membrane (OMM) at baseline. However, BAX is externalized to the plasma membrane / outer leaflet during apoptosis and other forms of programmed cell death (apoptosis, necroptosis, pyroptosis). During apoptosis, BAX translocates from the cytosol to the OMM, and dying/apoptotic cells expose BAX and other mitochondrial-membrane proteins on the outer face of the plasma membrane as a damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP). This apoptosis-driven surface display qualifies as cell_state_induced contextualism. Additionally, BAX has documented immunogenicity in cancer settings where dying tumor cells present BAX epitopes; CAR-T and TCR-based programs have been explored targeting BAX-derived peptides, though these are pMHC-restricted. The baseline verdict is 'no' / cytoplasmic, but the cell-death-induced surface pool moves the call to 'contextual'.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Unclear whether BAX's externalization during apoptosis is sufficiently stable and surface-directed (as opposed to transient exposure during cell disruption) to justify 'contextual' rather than 'no'. BAX is not a canonical surface protein, and most apoptotic-cell BAX exposure may be an artifact of membrane disruption rather than active translocation to the outer leaflet. However, documented immunogenicity and DAMP presentation in apoptotic-cell biology supports at least a 'contextual' call for cell-death-state biology.
